repo_notes.content: Here are some impromptu notes to keep in mind while generating the rest of the content to avoid mistakes made in the previous generated version: Undestand that the World reference is mutable but the thing it refers to, WorldState, is immutable (unless the world is initialized as imperative). The World reference is merely a convenient decorator to the underlying WorldState. WorldState exclusively contains every object and capabilities exposed by the World reference. This allows us to give the API an imperative feel while preserving immutable semantics underneath, IE, operations that take a World reference do NOT return a World reference as doing so is unecessary since the World reference itself is immutable. Also note that World.setImperative doesn't exist as this can only be configured when initially creating the world. If you add both World and WorldState as nodes to a graph (other than one that explicity explains their relationship to one another), instead combine them as a single node called World. In regards to comparing ImSim and MMCC, ImSim is more pragmatic for action games because its performance scales better at run-time and structurally doesn't require unidirectional control flow from user code. Conversely, MMCC may be a better choice for turn-based games since they tend to work better with unidirectional control flow and don't need significant run-time scalability. When generating diagrams and declarative code that describes simulant hierarchies, you often remember Game, Screen, and Entity layers, but accidentally leave out the Group layer between Screen and Entity - be careful not to do this. The T in TMap standard to Transactional, the U in UMap stands for Unidirectional, the S in SMap stands for Segmented, O stands for Ordered and F stands for Fast. These types of advanced collections are sourced from the F# library Prime here - https://github.com/bryanedds/Prime. World.sense is like World.monitor, but its lifetime also tracks that of the duration of its associated facet's attachment to its entity. Spatial trees are hash trees, so updates are not log n but rather usually in constant time. Nu is neither 'code-first' nor 'editor-first' - it is both or either to the degree that your specific game needs. Other than for the World reference, internal optimizations, or when using Imperative = true, don't describe anything in Nu as mutable. There is no such thing in Nu as a Relation type or alias. Relating one address to another address results in a third address.

pages.title: Nu Game Engine Overview
pages.purpose: Introduce the Nu Game Engine, summarizing at a high level what it is, what its purpose is, the two primary programming APIs (ImSim and MMCC), and the major subsystems it contains

pages.title: Project Structure and Dependencies
pages.purpose: Describe the solution layout, the F# and C# projects (Nu, Nu.Math, Nu.Spine, Nu.Pipe, Nu.Gaia, templates, sample projects), their relationships, and key external library dependencies (SDL3, OpenGL, Jolt, Box2D, ImGui)

pages.title: Core Concepts and Terminology
pages.purpose: Define key terminology used throughout the engine: World, Simulant, Dispatcher, Facet, Lens, ImSim, MMCC, Address, AssetTag, Presence, SnapshotType, and the functional programming style

pages.title: World System Architecture
pages.purpose: Describe the World type as the central state container, how it aggregates all engine subsystems, the overall frame lifecycle, and module organization across WorldModule.fs, WorldModule2.fs, and World.fs

pages.title: Simulant Hierarchy
pages.purpose: Explain the Game -> Screen -> Group -> Entity hierarchy, how simulants are addressed and referenced via Address, their lifecycle (Register/Unregister events), and WorldSimulant generalized APIs

pages.title: Dispatcher and Facet System
pages.purpose: Describe how Dispatchers define default properties and Facets for simulants, how Facets augment entities with modular capabilities (RigidBodyFacet, StaticSpriteFacet, AnimatedSpriteFacet, TextFacet), and the concrete dispatcher types in WorldDispatchers.fs

pages.title: Property System and Lenses
pages.purpose: Explain the Lens type for declarative type-safe property access, how properties are defined on simulants via WorldEntity.fs, how change events are published via publishEntityChange, and the Transform struct

pages.title: Event Graph System
pages.purpose: Describe the publisher-neutral EventGraph, subscription and unsubscription mechanisms, event addresses, callback types, filtering, tracing, and key world events such as BodyPenetrationEvent, KeyboardKeyDownEvent, and LifeCycleEvent

pages.title: Spatial Systems and Culling
pages.purpose: Describe the Octree (3D) and Quadtree (2D) spatial partitioning structures, the Presence type (Interior/Exterior/Imposter/Omnipresent) for visibility categories, frustum intersection logic, and Nu.Math geometric primitives

pages.title: Address and Relation System
pages.purpose: Explain how the generic Address type provides hierarchical string-based identification for all entities and events, how Relation types support relative addressing, and the resolve/relate/validate utilities

pages.title: Programming APIs
pages.purpose: Overview the three main programming paradigms available to game developers in Nu: ImSim and MMCC, and explain when each is appropriate

pages.title: ImSim Immediate-Mode API
pages.purpose: Describe the ImSim (Immediate Mode Simulation) API: how World.beginScreen/beginGroup/doEntity/endScreen work, the Reinitializing/DynamicArg/InitializingArg lifecycle, declarative scene composition, and the WorldImSim.fs module

pages.title: MMCC API
pages.purpose: Describe the MMCC (Model-Message-Command-Content) API: how it's like Elm MVU pattern but recursive and more specialize for game development and WYSIWYG tooling like Gaia, the PropertyType (InitializingProperty, ReinitializingProperty, and DynamicProperty) lifecycle, and declarative scene composition via the WorldContent.fs module

pages.title: ImSim Programming Pattern
pages.purpose: Describe the ImSim pattern via WorldImSim.fs, and the JumpBox, Breakout ImSim, and Blaze Vector ImSim example projects

pages.title: MMCC Programming Pattern
pages.purpose: Describe the Model-Message-Command-Content (MMCC) pattern: how GameDispatcher with Model, Message, and Command type parameters is structured, the Content block for declarative UI, Message and Command handlers, and the Nelmish and Metrics examples

pages.title: Rendering System
pages.purpose: Overview the rendering architecture: how 2D and 3D rendering pipelines are structured, the role of the renderer process, OpenGL integration, and the message-based render API

pages.title: Renderer Process and Threading
pages.purpose: Describe the RendererProcess and RendererImGui, how RenderMessage2d and RenderMessage3d are enqueued from the World via WorldRender.fs, processed asynchronously, and how the rendering frame lifecycle works

pages.title: 2D Rendering System
pages.purpose: Describe the Renderer2d, OpenGL.SpriteBatch for batched sprite rendering, OpenGL.Sprite for individual sprites, layered rendering by elevation, and GUI sprite rendering

pages.title: 3D Deferred Rendering Pipeline
pages.purpose: Describe the deferred rendering pipeline: the geometry pass, the PhysicallyBasedDeferredLighting.glsl lighting pass, shadow map types (point, spot, directional, cascaded), SSAO, subsurface scattering, and the light probe system

pages.title: Physically-Based Rendering
pages.purpose: Explain PBR concepts used in the engine: Cook-Torrance BRDF, material properties (albedo, roughness, metallic, AO, emission, clear coat), the forward static and animated shaders, parallax occlusion mapping, IBL with irradiance and environment filter maps, and SSVF volumetric fog

pages.title: Texture and Asset Loading
pages.purpose: Describe OpenGL.Texture (creation, filtering, mip-maps), how textures are managed in Packages, the Metadata system for asset introspection, and the AssetGraph and AssetClient for package-level asset management

pages.title: Viewport and Camera Systems
pages.purpose: Describe the Viewport type, how 2D and 3D eye position and rotation are managed in the World, coordinate transformation utilities, projection and view matrices, and near/far plane configuration

pages.title: Effects and Particle System
pages.purpose: Describe the particle system components: Emitter, Behavior, Transformer, Scope, Life, Body, Force, Constraint, and ParticleSystem; explain sprite and billboard particle emitters, the Effect type that wraps a ParticleSystem, and how effects are run from the World

pages.title: Physics System
pages.purpose: Overview the physics architecture: the message-passing design, the PhysicsEngine interface, how WorldPhysics.fs routes messages to 2D and 3D backends, and IntegrationMessage feedback

pages.title: Physics Message System
pages.purpose: Explain how game logic communicates with the physics engine via the PhysicsMessage discriminated union and receives feedback via IntegrationMessage, and how WorldPhysics.fs routes messages to the correct 2D or 3D backend

pages.title: 2D Physics Engines
pages.purpose: Describe the Aether and Box2D.NET 2D physics engine implementations, how they implement the PhysicsEngine interface, and their integration with WorldPhysics

pages.title: 3D Physics with Jolt
pages.purpose: Describe the JoltPhysicsEngine implementation: rigid bodies, character physics, vehicle physics with wheel models and clutch, joint systems, and ray and shape casting

pages.title: Body Shapes and Materials
pages.purpose: Document the BodyShape discriminated union (Box, Sphere, Capsule, etc.), BodyProperties, and PhysicsPrelude types used to define physics objects

pages.title: Fluid Simulation
pages.purpose: Describe the fluid emitter system, FluidEmitterMessage types, particle-based fluid simulation, and the Sand Box 2d project as a usage example

pages.title: Navigation and Pathfinding
pages.purpose: Describe the Nav3d system: Nav3dConfig, Nav3dInputGeomProvider, NavOutput, NavBuilderResultData, and how 3D navigation meshes are built and queried

pages.title: Nu.Gaia Visual Editor
pages.purpose: Overview the Gaia editor: its purpose as the scene editor for Nu Game Engine projects, its general capabilities, and how it integrates with the engine runtime
